result:
I keep thinking about the way light looks when it’s almost gone—how it clings to the edges of things like it’s afraid to leave. A few nights ago I stood at the kitchen sink washing a single bowl, and the window faced west, so the last scrap of sunset slid across the neighbor’s rusted gutters and turned them into molten gold for maybe thirty seconds. Then it was just oxidized metal again. I don’t know why that feels like the most honest transaction I’ve witnessed in months: the world briefly pretending to be precious metal, the sky taking its costume back without apology.

We’re supposed to accumulate evidence that we matter—photographs, promotions, the expanding circumference of our waistlines or our LinkedIn networks—but lately I’ve been craving subtraction. Last week I gave away the thick stack of birthday cards I’d saved since I was seven. I thought I’d feel hollowed out, but instead the drawer they’d occupied tasted like oxygen when I opened it. Oxygen and cedar. It turns out memory is lighter when it isn’t laminated.

Yesterday I listened to a physicist explain that the universe is mostly made of something we can’t see and haven’t named, something that refuses to interact with our instruments except by implication. She called it “the missing mass,” but her voice curled around the phrase like it was a lullaby, not a crisis. I keep replaying the interview because I like how calmly she admits that the largest presence in existence is intangible, that the ledger of reality is balanced by a question mark we carry in our coat pockets without noticing the weight.

Maybe that’s why I’ve started pocketing actual stones on my walks—grey ones veined with quartz, smooth basins of basalt that fit the hollow of my palm. I carry them for a block or two, then set them down somewhere conspicuous: on a mailbox, the lip of a bus stop, the threshold of a stranger’s porch. A geologic relay race whose finish line is “elsewhere.” Each time I let go I feel the same small tug, like releasing the string of a kite that will never stop climbing. The stones don’t care; they’ve already spent eons learning how to wait.

At night the city brushes its teeth with emergency vehicles. Sirens doppler past my open window, red comets that leave no tail, and I think: somewhere a heart is being shocked back into rhythm, a lung re-inflated, a story rerouted mid-sentence. The paramedics will pack up their plastic wrappers, the street will reabsorb its quiet, and tomorrow commuters will walk over the same asphalt without realizing it was recently a stage. Most of us never get to read the revised drafts of the nights we almost left.

I read that the human brain edits memory each time we retrieve it, the way a librarian might re-shelve a book with a few pages subtly rewritten. This means the past is biodegradable, composting inside us faster than we can bury it. I find that encouraging, like discovering the warranty on heartbreak expires without warning. Maybe the version of my father who exists in my head will someday forget the exact timbre of his disappointment; maybe the kitchen where my mother cried into a dish-towel will blur until only the dish-towel remains, pastel daisies blooming through the cotton like stubborn wildflowers after a forest fire.

Tonight I turned off every lamp and let the apartment fill with the blue glow that leaks in from the highway. Objects emerged in that dim currency: the lampshade, the leaning tower of unread books, the houseplant that refuses to die no matter how inconsistently I water it. They looked like extras in a movie about someone else’s life, patient and unpaid. I stood there long enough to become one of them, an uncredited silhouette waiting for direction. No one arrived to yell “cut,” so I stayed until my eyes adjusted and the room grew limbs made of my own breathing.

Somewhere in the dark the stones I didn’t keep are cooling under a stranger’s touch. Somewhere the missing mass holds galaxies together like a rumor everyone pretends to believe. Somewhere light is apologizing to a gutter for ever calling it gold. And I am here with the bowl already dry, the drawer still tasting of cedar, the sirens gone shy in the distance. All of it perfectly weightless, all of it too heavy to hold.
